framework_name: AFlow (paper: Automating Agentic Workflow Generation via Search)
maintainer_org: FoundationAgents (Jiayi Zhang & Jinyu Xiang co-first; Chenglin Wu / DeepWisdom and Yuyu Luo / HKUST(GZ) co-corresponding). DeepWisdom + HKUST(GZ) + Renmin University + Nanjing University + Fudan + KAUST + Universite de Montreal/Mila + HKUST.
authors: Jiayi Zhang*, Jinyu Xiang*, Zhaoyang Yu, Fengwei Teng, Xiong-Hui Chen, Jiaqi Chen, Mingchen Zhuge, Xin Cheng, Sirui Hong, Jinlin Wang, Bingnan Zheng, Bang Liu, Yuyu Luo†, Chenglin Wu† (14 authors; * equal contribution, † corresponding)
license: (repo: see github.com/FoundationAgents/AFlow; integrated into MetaGPT ecosystem — typically MIT)
execution_kernel_pattern: n/a — paper proposing workflow search (not a runtime kernel). AFlow is a meta-framework that automatically discovers agentic workflows; the discovered workflows themselves are code-represented graphs of LLM-invoking Nodes (each Node parameterized by Model M, Prompt P, Temperature tau, Output Format F in {xml,json,markdown,raw}) connected by code-represented Edges. Search algorithm is a Monte Carlo Tree Search variant over the workflow code space: each tree node is a complete workflow; iteration cycle = Soft Mixed Probability Selection -> LLM-Based Expansion (Claude-3.5-Sonnet as optimizer mutates workflow code) -> Execution Evaluation (5 runs on validation set, mean+stddev) -> Experience Backpropagation. Selection formula: P_mixed(i) = lambda*(1/n) + (1-lambda)*softmax(alpha*(s_i - s_max)) with lambda=0.2, alpha=0.4. Search space pruned by fixing M, tau, F and constraining mutations to use predefined Operators.

multi_agent_support.style: n/a — paper produces multi-agent workflows; AFlow itself is not multi-agent
multi_agent_support.notes: The workflows AFlow discovers can be multi-agent (e.g., MultiPersona Debate-like ensembles emerge autonomously even without operators per the ablation on GSM8K). AFlow is an outer optimization loop over agentic workflow space, not a runtime.

tool_protocol: n/a — paper; Operators serve as the tool abstraction within discovered workflows (e.g., Programmer Operator executes generated Python with a timeout via concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor).
memory_model: Tree-structured experience: each MCTS tree node stores (1) the workflow's performance score, (2) the optimizer's modification relative to parent, (3) success/failure tag relative to parent. Experience is backpropagated up the tree and fed into the LLM optimizer's expansion prompt so past successes are reused and past failures avoided. Distinct from ADAS's linear-list experience store, which AFlow argues is the reason ADAS underperforms.
observability_story: Per-iteration execution evaluation logged (mean + stddev over 5 runs on validation set). Tree visualization of optimization path published in paper Figure 6 for GSM8K case study (rounds 1-20 with score deltas and modification descriptions).
typical_use_case: Automatically generate optimized agentic workflows for reasoning tasks with numerical evaluation functions (code, math, QA). Demonstrated across HumanEval, MBPP, GSM8K, MATH lv5*, HotpotQA, DROP. Particularly useful for making weaker/cheaper models match stronger models on the cost-performance Pareto front.
maturity_signal: Research artifact, paper-bound. ICLR 2025 oral acceptance; arXiv v4 dated 2025-04-15. Code on github.com/FoundationAgents/AFlow. Same team's MetaGPT is the production-grade companion (68.3k stars). Heavily cited as the workflow-search baseline in 2025 agent literature.
one_paragraph_differentiator: AFlow is the first work to frame agentic workflow design as a search problem over code-represented workflow graphs and to apply MCTS efficiently within it. Where prior automated workflow methods either constrain the search space (GPTSwarm: graph-only; DSPy/TextGrad: prompt-only within a fixed workflow) or use inefficient search (ADAS: linear heuristic over code), AFlow combines (a) code as the most expressive edge representation, (b) reusable Operators that compress common agentic patterns into search-space primitives, and (c) tree-structured experience replay through an MCTS variant that uses an LLM (Claude-3.5-Sonnet) as the expansion operator. Empirically it averages +5.7% over six manually-designed prompting-method baselines and +19.5% over the prior best automated workflow method (ADAS), with the headline economic claim being that a workflow AFlow discovers using GPT-4o-mini, when executed on DeepSeek-V2.5, matches GPT-4o IO on HumanEval at 4.55% of the dollar cost.
benchmarks_evaluated: MATH lv5* (617 problems, level-5 only, 4 categories: Combinatorics&Probability, Number Theory, Pre-algebra, Pre-calculus; Solve Rate %)
baselines_compared: ADAS (Hu et al. 2024) — the only other automated workflow optimization baseline

improvement_claims.vs_ADAS_on_hard_tasks: +57% relative on MATH lv5* and MBPP (paper's wording — actually +59% on MATH 35.4->56.2 and +56% on MBPP 53.4->83.4)

limitations_authors_flag: AFlow is scoped to reasoning tasks with numerical evaluation functions G — tasks without an explicit programmatic grader are out of scope as formulated.
Search space is simplified by fixing model M, temperature tau, and output format F (model selection and temperature are NOT searched in this work).

Workflows discovered with one executor model do not transfer optimally to other models — Table 2 shows the workflow searched with DeepSeek-V2.5 performs notably weaker on GPT-4o-mini than the workflow searched with GPT-4o-mini itself, suggesting per-model workflow re-search is needed.

MCTS still requires 20 iteration rounds with multiple validation runs per round (5 runs/iteration), which is compute-expensive — the paper does not claim search itself is cheap, only that the discovered workflow is cheap to execute.

Operator set is human-designed (7 operators extracted from existing literature). The ablation shows AFlow without operators still works (93.1% on GSM8K vs the full version), but the operators are not learned end-to-end.
Empirical scope is six reasoning benchmarks; transfer to open-ended creative or interactive (browser, OS) tasks is not demonstrated.
does_not_compare_against: Crucially, AFlow does NOT benchmark MetaGPT, AutoGen, or ChatDev as named baselines despite being authored by the MetaGPT team. The only multi-agent framework cited (MetaGPT, in references only) is not in Table 1. AutoGen and ChatDev are not mentioned anywhere in the paper body. Cross-framework numerical comparison of these three frameworks under their own names does NOT exist in this paper — the cycle-7 cross_framework_2026_05.md assumption that 'three of the 13 [frameworks] appear as baselines' in AFlow is INCORRECT and must be revised.
verified_paper_baselines_actual: IO, CoT, CoT-SC, MedPrompt, MultiPersona Debate, Self-Refine, ADAS (workflow-optimization method, not a framework). All are prompting strategies or workflow-search methods, NOT named multi-agent frameworks.
analysis_depth: deep — full PDF extracted (38 pages, pypdf), all section 5 results tables read directly including Table 1 (main results), Table 2 (HumanEval transferability), and Appendix D (Pareto cost analysis). Authors / affiliations / venue verified from page 1. Limitations and conclusion verified from pages 8-10. Critical finding: paper does NOT name-check MetaGPT/AutoGen/ChatDev as baselines, contradicting the cycle-7 benchmark survey assumption.
known_anti_patterns: Applying AFlow to tasks without programmatic graders (open-ended writing, interactive web) — the entire formulation requires a numerical G.
Assuming the discovered workflow transfers across executor LLMs without re-search (Table 2 shows transferability is partial).

Treating AFlow as a multi-agent framework benchmark — it is a meta-optimization paper that benchmarks against prompting techniques (CoT, MedPrompt, Self-Refine) and one workflow-search peer (ADAS), not against named multi-agent frameworks.
